---
title: "MCU knockout: mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake"
subtitle: "Platelet whole-cell model — demo experiment 3 of 4"
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: 2026-06-23
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    geometry: "margin=2.5cm"
    fontsize: 11pt
    mainfont: "STIX Two Text"
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: blue
    urlcolor: blue
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    include-in-header: ../pandoc-header.tex
  html:
    toc: true
    embed-resources: true
---

## Purpose

Knock out the **mitochondrial calcium uniporter (MCU)** on the
[baseline experiment](1-baseline-resting-agonist.qmd) and examine its effect on
the cytosolic Ca²⁺ transient. This experiment is most useful as an **honest check
against the literature**: the model's simplified treatment of MCU gives a result
that, compared with platelet-specific MCU-knockout data, turns out to be the
**wrong way round** — a clear illustration of the limits of buffer-only
mitochondrial handling in a single-cell model.

## What is changed from the baseline

Exactly **one model setting** changes from the baseline:

| | Baseline | MCU knockout |
|---|---|---|
| `mcu_vmax_scale` | 1.0 | **0.0** |

Everything else is identical (Dolan agonist at 60 s, +Ca²⁺, 300 s).
`mcu_vmax_scale` (a parameter in `reconstruction/platelet/run_config.py`) scales
the maximal MCU flux, so `0.0` removes mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake entirely. The
exact commands are in the [Appendix](#appendix).

## Biological reasoning

In the model, MCU is represented essentially as a **cytosolic Ca²⁺ buffer /
sink**: during activation it carries a share of the Ca²⁺ released from the store
into the mitochondrial matrix, shaping the cytosolic signal. Viewed purely as a
buffer, the expectation is straightforward — remove the sink and the buffered
species (cytosolic Ca²⁺) should **rise**. There is nothing counterintuitive
about that direction; it is simply what removing a buffer does.

Real platelet MCU does considerably more than buffer cytosolic Ca²⁺:
mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake drives bioenergetics and reactive-oxygen-species (ROS)
signalling and — as the measurements below show — also *supports* store-operated
Ca²⁺ entry. The model captures only the buffer role, and that omission is what
the comparison exposes.

## What happens in the model

Removing MCU **raises** the cytosolic Ca²⁺ peak from **429 nM to 602 nM (+40 %)**
and the sustained plateau likewise (423 → 600 nM) (@fig-mcu); the DTS store still
empties to ~1 µM in both cases. This is exactly the buffer expectation — with no
mitochondrial sink, more of the released Ca²⁺ stays free in the cytosol. The
slightly higher integrin activation (PAC-1⁺ 0.796 → 0.842) and thromboxane that
follow are simply downstream of the larger Ca²⁺ signal. The result is a direct,
self-consistent consequence of the model's MCU-as-buffer assumption — not an
independent or emergent prediction.

![**Model prediction — cytosolic Ca²⁺ with MCU knocked out (current) vs the baseline (grey).** Left: in the model, removing mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake raises the cytosolic peak/plateau by ~40 % (429 → 602 nM) — the curve sits *above* the grey wild-type baseline. Centre: the DTS store empties to ~1 µM in both runs (grey and current overlap at the bottom), so the mitochondria redistribute Ca²⁺ away from the cytosol rather than returning it to the store; store-operated entry (right axis) is similar. Right: IP₃ is essentially unchanged (the perturbation is downstream of IP₃). This is the model's prediction; the *Comparison* section below shows it runs opposite to the platelet MCU-knockout literature. The baseline's duration is shown in its legend label.](../figures/demos/mcu_calcium.png){#fig-mcu width=100%}

## What happens in real platelets

The platelet-specific MCU-knockout study — Ajanel et al., *Circ. Res.* 2025;
137(4):474–492 (Mcu^plt−/−^ mice) — found the **opposite** direction for
cytosolic Ca²⁺:

- **Cytosolic Ca²⁺ was reduced, not raised.** Agonist-evoked Ca²⁺ store release
  and store-operated Ca²⁺ entry were both *significantly reduced* in MCU⁻/⁻
  platelets, "independent of agonist" — explicitly including the GPCR agonists
  thrombin and the ADP analogue 2-MeSADP that this experiment uses.
- **Mitochondrial Ca²⁺ flux** was reduced only under **ITAM** agonists
  (collagen/GPVI, CLEC-2); under **GPCR** agonists it was unchanged, and
  GPCR-induced aggregation was unchanged.
- **Mechanism.** MCU's functional role was attributed to mitochondrial
  **ROS → Syk/PLCγ2 (ITAM) signalling**, plus an unexpected role in supporting
  **Orai1/STIM1-mediated store-operated entry**. The authors call the link
  between MCU loss and lower cytosolic Ca²⁺ "unclear," and note the prior
  expectation (Brass, 1984) was that blocking mitochondrial uptake leaves
  cytosolic Ca²⁺ essentially **unchanged**.

(The cytosolic-Ca²⁺ findings are reported directionally, in supplemental figures;
no resting or peak nM values are given, so there is no magnitude to fit against —
but the *sign* is unambiguous.)

## Honest comparison

- **Direction is wrong.** The model *raises* cytosolic Ca²⁺ on MCU loss; the data
  show it *fall* (reduced store release and SOCE).
- **Agonist-dependence is wrong.** The model's effect is produced under a GPCR
  (thrombin/ADP/ATP) mix. In the data the cytosolic reduction was independent of
  agonist, and the GPCR *sparing* applied only to mitochondrial flux and
  aggregation — not to cytosolic Ca²⁺.
- **Mechanism is incomplete.** The model knows MCU only as a cytosolic buffer.
  The real net effect is dominated by MCU's *support* of store-operated entry (so
  its loss *lowers* cytosolic Ca²⁺) and by mitochondrial-ROS/ITAM signalling —
  neither of which is in the model.
- **What is right.** Given its assumptions, the model's behaviour is internally
  consistent (remove a buffer → the buffered species rises), and "the store
  empties regardless" is physically sound. The assumptions are simply too narrow.
- **A correction.** Earlier versions of this note (and related write-ups)
  described this as a "counterintuitive, emergent, literature-matched" result and
  cited "Ghatge 2026." On checking the verifiable platelet-MCU literature
  (Ajanel et al., 2025) that claim does not hold; the comparison and the citation
  are corrected here.

## Take-away

This is the most instructive **negative** result in the set. The model's MCU
knockout is a clean, self-consistent consequence of treating the uniporter as a
pure cytosolic Ca²⁺ buffer — but the actual platelet MCU-knockout phenotype runs
the other way, because MCU's dominant roles (supporting store-operated entry, and
ITAM-specific mitochondrial-ROS signalling) are absent from the model. Two
lessons follow: emergent-looking predictions must be checked against data before
they are claimed; and there is concrete future work here — adding mitochondrial
ROS and the GPVI/ITAM pathway, and coupling MCU to store-operated entry — so that
mitochondrial Ca²⁺ handling reflects more than buffering. This is logged as
GitHub issue #76.

## Provenance

The simulations were produced by `runscripts/manual/runFromConfig.py`, and the
figure by `runscripts/manual/analysisPlatelet.py --plot demo_calcium` (figure
module `models/platelet/analysis/single/demo_calcium.py`, reading the
`CalciumTrace` listener; the grey baseline overlay is supplied through the
`PLATELET_BASELINE_SIMOUT` environment variable). Both runs used **seed 0** for
**300 s** on Python 3.11.5, at git commit **`9724cc81`** — the exact commit is
also stamped in the figure footnote and in each run's `metadata/metadata.json`.

## Appendix — reproduce {#appendix}

From the repository root (`PYTHONPATH=$PWD`):

```bash
# Baseline run (provides the grey overlay)
cat > base.json <<'EOF'
{"length_sec": 300, "seed": 0, "run_config": {"agonist_delay_s": 60}}
EOF
python runscripts/manual/runFromConfig.py --config base.json --out exp_base

# MCU knockout: mitochondrial Ca2+ uptake removed
cat > mcu.json <<'EOF'
{"length_sec": 300, "seed": 0,
 "run_config": {"agonist_delay_s": 60, "mcu_vmax_scale": 0.0}}
EOF
python runscripts/manual/runFromConfig.py --config mcu.json --out exp_mcu

# Figure: MCU knockout (coloured) over the baseline (grey)
base=out/exp_base/platelet_stub_000000/000000/generation_000000/000000/simOut
PLATELET_BASELINE_SIMOUT=$base MPLBACKEND=Agg \
  python runscripts/manual/analysisPlatelet.py exp_mcu --plot demo_calcium
```

The figure module `demo_calcium.py` plots the `CalciumTrace` columns `ca_cyt_nM`,
`ca_dts_uM`, `soce_flux_nMs` and `ip3_nM`.
